terms.definition: An object representing an error or unusual event that interrupts normal control flow and propagates until caught.
The statement that runs risky code in a try block and handles matching errors in one or more except blocks.
The statement that triggers an exception, optionally with a message, signaling that something went wrong.
The inheritance tree of error types rooted at BaseException; catching a base type also catches its subclasses.
A block attached to try that always runs, whether or not an exception occurred — used for cleanup.
A block attached to try that runs only if the try body completed without raising an exception.
A user-defined error type created by subclassing Exception, letting callers catch a specific failure precisely.
Linking a new exception to the one that caused it using `raise NewError(...) from original`, preserving the full cause in the traceback.
"Easier to Ask Forgiveness than Permission" — the Pythonic style of attempting an operation and handling exceptions rather than pre-checking.
The report Python prints when an uncaught exception propagates, showing the call stack and the line where the error occurred.
